title: Spectral Analysis Chamber
description: The user operates an antique spectroscopy camera to analyze the iridescent shell of an abalone, identifying chemical compounds responsible for its nacre layers. By adjusting wavelength filters, focal distance, and exposure parameters on the camera, the user isolates specific elemental signatures (calcium carbonate, aragonite, conchiolin) and logs them into a periodic table panel to complete the analysis.
prompt:
Create a single HTML file using Three.js r170 that implements an interactive spectral analysis lab. Load AntiqueCamera.glb on the left side of the scene and IridescenceAbalone.glb on the right, placed on a dark lab bench surface (a simple box geometry with dark metallic material). Use a PerspectiveCamera at position (0, 3, 7) looking at the origin. Add ambient light (intensity 0.3), a warm point light near the camera model, and a cool directional light from above. Implement OrbitControls limited to a 60-degree arc. Between the two models, render a visible beam (a thin cyan cylinder) connecting the camera lens area to the abalone, representing the spectroscopy beam. Create an HTML overlay panel on the right with these interactive controls: (1) a wavelength slider from 380nm to 780nm that changes the beam color accordingly using visible spectrum mapping, (2) a focal distance slider that scales the beam width, (3) an exposure dial with values Low/Medium/High that adjusts beam opacity, (4) a polarization toggle (S/P) that rotates the abalone model 45 degrees when switched, (5) a capture button that reads current parameters and checks if they match one of three target compound signatures. Define three compounds: CaCO3 (wavelength 480-520nm, Medium exposure, S polarization), Aragonite (wavelength 580-620nm, High exposure, P polarization), Conchiolin (wavelength 400-440nm, Low exposure, S polarization). When capture is clicked with correct parameters within range, highlight the abalone with an emissive glow matching the beam color, add the compound name to a results list on the panel, and show a success toast. Track discovered compounds and display a completion message with a particle burst effect (simple sprite particles) when all three are found. Validate that each compound can only be logged once. The abalone should slowly rotate on its Y-axis by default. Add a reset button that clears all discovered compounds and resets all sliders.

[State Exposure (required)]
Expose window.__3D_STATE__ updated every animation frame:
window.__3D_STATE__ = {
  wavelengthNm: number,         // current wavelength setting in nanometers (380-780)
  focalDistance: number,         // focal distance parameter affecting beam width (0.5-2.0)
  exposureLevel: number,         // exposure setting as numeric index (0=Low, 1=Medium, 2=High)
  polarizationMode: string,         // current polarization mode, either 'S' or 'P'
  beamOpacity: number,         // calculated opacity of spectroscopy beam (0.0-1.0)
  beamWidth: number,         // current width/scale of the beam cylinder
  abaloneRotation: number,         // rotation angle of abalone in degrees (0 or 45)
  captureCount: number,         // total number of successful captures performed
  lastCaptureValid: boolean,         // whether the most recent capture matched a target signature
  compoundDetected: string,         // name of detected compound or empty string if none
  cameraLightIntensity: number,         // intensity of the point light near camera (0.0-1.0)
  beamColorHex: string,         // hex color code of the spectroscopy beam based on wavelength
}
